experiments.title: Synthesis of Al‑doped precursor (PCAM) by co‑precipitation

extra.raw_process: Aqueous solution of NiSO4, CoSO4, MnSO4 (2 mol∙L‑1) mixed with 4 M NaOH and ammonia in a stainless‑steel reactor under N2; Al2(SO4)3 solution added; greenish powders dried at 110 °C under vacuum.

measurements.snippet: Elemental analysis performed by atomic emission spectroscopy measurements showed that formula units of PCAMs are … Ni 0.86 Co 0.07 Mn 0.05 Al 0.009 (OH) 2 for NCMA.

conclusions.text: The dopant retains completely during lithiation process.
conclusions.snippet: During lithiation process the dopant retains completely.

experiments.snippet: The precursors of cathode material (PCAM) were prepared via employing a controlled co‑precipitation reaction in a continuous stirred reactor.

experiments.title: Calcination of precursors to obtain cathode materials

extra.raw_process: Calcination of PCAM with LiOH·H2O powder (molar ratio 1:1.08) at 750 °C for 12 h and then at 650 °C for 4 h in pure O2 atmosphere.

measurements.snippet: The resulted CAMs demonstrated almost similar diffraction peaks (Fig. 1b) which are corresponded to the LiNiO2 R3m phase (ICCD No01‑084‑8629).
Inclusion of Al 3+ ions in the material crystal lattice led to the increasing the interlayer distances which is observed from the shifting the peak at 21°.

conclusions.text: Molar ratios of the components remain unchanged during calcination.
conclusions.snippet: Molar ratios of the components remain unchanged during calcination with regard to the precursors, so the formula units are LiNi0.86Co0.1Mn0.04O2 and LiNi0.86Co0.07Mn0.05Al0.009O2, respectively.

experiments.snippet: Cathode materials were prepared by calcination of the PCAM with LiOH·H2O powder in molar ratio of 1:1.08 at 750 °C for 12 h and 650 °C for 4 h in the pure oxygen atmosphere.

experiments.title: Electrochemical performance test of Al‑doped cathode (NCMA)

extra.raw_process: Galvanostatic charge‑discharge cycling at various C‑rates.

measurements.snippet: The obtained material LiNi0.86Co0.07Mn0.05Al0.009O2 demonstrated remarkable performance (205 and 151 mA∙h∙g‑1 at 0.1 C and 2 C).
The obtained material LiNi0.86Co0.07Mn0.05Al0.009O2 demonstrated remarkable performance (205 and 151 mA∙h∙g‑1 at 0.1 C and 2 C).

measurements.quantity_kind: Capacity retention after 100 cycles at 1 C
measurements.snippet: The cyclic stability of the Al‑doped cathode material was 83% after 100 cycles at 1 C.

conclusions.text: Al‑doping enhances high‑rate performance and cyclic stability.
conclusions.snippet: The doped cathode materials were demonstrated superior high‑rate performance (150 mAh∙g‑1 at 2 C) as well as enhanced cyclic stability at 1 C current.

experiments.snippet: The doped cathode materials were demonstrated superior high‑rate performance (150 mAh∙g‑1 at 2 C) as well as enhanced cyclic stability at 1 C current.

claims.text: Al‑doping can effectively enhance the rate of lithium‑ion intercalation or deintercalation due to enlarging the interlayer distances.
claims.snippet: The excellent performance demonstrates that Al‑doping can effectively enhance the rate of lithium ions intercalation or deintercalation due to enlarging the interlayer distances.

claims.text: Doping of precursors is an easy and effective route to enhance the functional properties of the cathode materials.
claims.snippet: the doping of precursors is ease and effective route to enhance the functional properties of the cathode materials.

claims.text: Al‑3+ ions improve the structural stability of cathode active material and reduce microcracking.
claims.snippet: Among the doping ions, Al 3+ ones improve the structural stability of CAM and reduce microcracking process.

claims.text: Nickel‑rich layered‑type cathodes have high theoretical and practical specific capacity (~200 mA∙h∙g‑1) and operating voltage >3.8 V vs. Li/Li+.
claims.snippet: high theoretical and practical (about 200 mA∙h∙g‑1) specific capacity, operating voltage (E > 3.8 V vs. Li/Li+).

claims.text: The most common method of adding dopant ions to the CAM structure is to introduce them at the precursor calcination stage in the presence of LiOH and a doping agent.
claims.snippet: The most common method of adding dopant ions to the CAM structure is to introduce them at the precursor calcination stage in the presence of LiOH and a doping agent.
